EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds increased across western and northern NM during the afternoon and evening hours of May 5th ahead of an unseasonably strong upper low moving into the Great Basin. During the overnight hours, winds turned westerly as a Pacific cold front raced east across the state. A few sites across northern New Mexico ended up reporting high wind gusts. After a brief lull during the mid to late morning hours of May 6th, deep daytime mixing and strong upper level winds at the base of the upper low over the central Rockies resulted in more high wind gusts during the afternoon hours across the northeast and central highlands and northeast plains. Winds tapered off after sunset, but remained elevated across the northern mountains during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton Municipal Airport measured a wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low continued to spin across the northern Rockies and northern Great Plains during the day on May 7th. Deep daytime mixing across New Mexico allowed stronger winds at the base of the upper low to mix down to the surface during the afternoon and early evening hours resulting in several high wind gusts across the northern mountains and surrounding highlands. Winds quickly tapered off after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public station at Kachina Peak in Taos Ski Valley measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January saw mostly near normal precipitation across northern and central NM on the whole, resulting in most areas seeing a static state in drought conditions. There were pockets of above normal precipitation focused over the northeastern plains, mainly Colfax and Union Counties, thanks to a blizzard that dumped heavy snow on the region in early January. Areas along the Continental Divide saw near to slightly above normal precipitation in west-central NM which led to a one or two categorical improvement in drought conditions there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought remained persistent across the northern third of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and well organized winter storm system crossed west to east across northern and central NM during January 4th. Heavy snow causing travel impacts to the I-40 corridor along and west of the Continental Divide began during the morning hours with snow expanding through the northern mountain and valley areas by midday. A mesoscale band of heavy snow developed over the west mesa of Albuquerque stretching down through the South Valley area by the afternoon period. This caused several inches to pile up in a short period of a few hours over southern and eastern portions of the ABQ metro area. Heavy snow was also reported through parts of the Sangre de Cristo and Sandia/Manzano Mountains. Numerous reports of severe driving conditions were reported by the NM Department of Transportation across western and northern NM with a few road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 11 inches of snow occurred at the Senorita Divide SNOTEL on the western high peaks of the Jemez Mountains. There were other reports of 6 to 8.5 inches along the rim of the caldera, and 3.3 to 5.6 inches in lower elevations near Los Alamos to Jemez Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow over northern and central NM at the southern edge of an upper level disturbance was observed on January 17th. Some of these high winds aloft crashed to the surface east of the central mountain chain producing sporadic high wind gusts at several locations during the morning hours. Westerly winds turned northwesterly on January 18th with high wind observations becoming isolated to western Guadalupe County during the morning hours. There were no reports of damage or injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January saw mostly near normal precipitation across northern and central NM on the whole, resulting in most areas seeing a static state in drought conditions. There were pockets of above normal precipitation focused over the northeastern plains, mainly Colfax and Union Counties, thanks to a blizzard that dumped heavy snow on the region in early January. Areas along the Continental Divide saw near to slightly above normal precipitation in west-central NM which led to a one or two categorical improvement in drought conditions there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions persisted across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong section of the jetstream screamed over the central mountain chain of NM on January 12th and 13th with mountain wave activity developing along and immediately east of the mountains. This acted to bring high winds aloft crashing to erratic locations along the adjacent highlands from Wagon Mound southward through the Vaughn and parts of remote eastern Lincoln County. In one instance, the high winds blew fresh snow that had fallen a few days earlier through Mora and Colfax Counties causing blowing snow drifts and an isolated ground blizzard. Snow drifts as high as vehicles developed along I-25 from the Fort Union exit to Springer, especially near Wagon Mound. NMDOT was forced to close this section of I-25 due to the ground blizzard conditions and snow drifts during the morning of January 13th. Thankfully there were no reports of accidents or damages. Otherwise, peak gusts of 58 to 77 mph were observed by spotters and automated weather stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SE Vaughn mesonet station measured a peak gust of 60 mph, with persistent high sustained winds of 37 to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and well organized winter storm system crossed west to east across northern and central NM on January 7th and exited into TX on January 8th. Heavy snow was observed across much of the western and northern mountains with travel impacts reported on area highways. Wrap around moisture combined with strong northerly winds gusts produced blizzard conditions through most of northeastern NM in an area between Raton, Clayton, Clovis, and Las Vegas. Reports of high winds gusting 60 to 70 mph and as high was 90 mph at Raton combined with blowing snow and snow drifts several feet high were collected in this area. Blizzard conditions resulted in a tragic multivehicle pileup along I-40 at Cuervo, NM east of Santa Rosa that unfortunately involved a loss of life. There were many road closures along both I-40 and I-25 as well as area highways across northeastern and east-central NM due to the high winds and blizzard conditions. Severe driving conditions and more isolated road closures were reported from heavy snow across western and northern NM. Snow amounts of one to two feet were reported atop the peaks of the northern mountains and at ski areas. High wind gusts of 62 to 66 mph were also observed at the Roswell and Sierra Blanca Airports. Key Impacts: road closures, traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The United States Forest Service Ranger Station in Cuba reported 6 inches of snow in Cuba.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January saw mostly near normal precipitation across northern and central NM on the whole, resulting in most areas seeing a static state in drought conditions. There were pockets of above normal precipitation focused over the northeastern plains, mainly Colfax and Union Counties, thanks to a blizzard that dumped heavy snow on the region in early January. Areas along the Continental Divide saw near to slightly above normal precipitation in west-central NM which led to a one or two categorical improvement in drought conditions there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought persisted across the zone with exceptional drought persisting over the south-central portion of the zone.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong section of the jetstream screamed over the central mountain chain of NM on January 12th and 13th with mountain wave activity developing along and immediately east of the mountains. This acted to bring high winds aloft crashing to erratic locations along the adjacent highlands from Wagon Mound southward through the Vaughn and parts of remote eastern Lincoln County. In one instance, the high winds blew fresh snow that had fallen a few days earlier through Mora and Colfax Counties causing blowing snow drifts and an isolated ground blizzard. Snow drifts as high as vehicles developed along I-25 from the Fort Union exit to Springer, especially near Wagon Mound. NMDOT was forced to close this section of I-25 due to the ground blizzard conditions and snow drifts during the morning of January 13th. Thankfully there were no reports of accidents or damages. Otherwise, peak gusts of 58 to 77 mph were observed by spotters and automated weather stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter just south of Mora reported high winds causing snow drifts across highways with estimated wind gust of 60 mph. A public weather station near Rociada also reported a 77 mph wind gust later in the evening of the 13th.

A storm spotter just west of Wagon Mound estimated gusts of 65 mph during this time.

CZ_NAME: JOHNSON AND BARTLETT MESAS INCLUDING RATON PASS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low stalled over Arizona Thursday, March 14th. Snow fell across far northeast New Mexico behind a backdoor cold front during the day. The cutoff upper low drew the backdoor front south and west through the gaps of the central mountain chain with snow filling in its wake that evening. High winds were observed at Albuquerque and the White Sands Missile Range for most of the day on the 15th. Peak gusts reached 60 to 70 mph immediately below the Tijeras Canyon along I-40 and to the Albuquerque Sunport, while also reaching 70 mph near the Oscura Mountains. The high winds caused damage to some parts of Albuquerque with roofs damaged and a recycling truck blown over. Thankfully, there were no fatalities or injuries reported. Meanwhile, significant snow resulted across the northern mountains Thursday night. A band of snow developed over western NM March 15th along the edge of the cold front, resulting in significant snow accumulations through the western and northern mountains Friday night into early Saturday morning, March 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton Port of Entry reported 6 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system crossed northern NM on March 24th with it's associated jet streak crossing much of southeastern NM. A strong Pacific cold front crossed west to east during the morning with gusty winds, and snow pushing through western and northern NM and into portions of the Rio Grande Valley during the morning. This front along with strong southwesterly flow brought high winds through most of the eastern plains for most of the day on the 24th. Numerous locations along and east of the central recorded peak wind gusts of 58 to 75 mph. Localized dust source regions were activated causing near zero visibility between Roswell and Artesia. This forced the closure of that stretch of U.S. Highway 285. Thankfully there were not reports of accidents. Heavy snow began over the northern mountains on March 24th, continuing over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and adjacent highlands through March 25th and into the early predawn hours of March 26th. Snow totals for the event were very impressive over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains in particular where the Taos and Santa Fe Ski resorts reported 40 and 34 inches respectively. Snow and blowing snow was reported along the U.S. 64 corridor in far northeastern NM between Raton and Clayton during the morning of March 25th resulting in severe winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pole 616 White Sands Missile Range mesonet station measured a peak gust of 62 mph. The Oscura Range mesonet station also measured a peak gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds ahead of a weakening Pacific upper low moved over New Mexico during the day Easter Sunday. Deep boundary layer mixing across the highlands and plains of eastern New Mexico allowed these strong winds aloft to mix down to the surface resulting in high sustained winds above 40 mph and gusts as high as 65 mph. Roswell observed a high wind gust of 63 mph resulting in blowing dust that reduced visibility to 6 miles. Winds tapered off just after sunset as the boundary layer decoupled.||Keywords: High Wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roswell ASOS observed a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms, highlighted by a slow-moving storm over the desert Southwest in the middle of the month, resulted in above average precipitation across northern, central, and western New Mexico. This resulted in the removal of extreme drought and the reduction of severe drought across north central New Mexico. Meanwhile, below average precipitation and periods of high winds across eastern New Mexico allowed severe to extreme drought conditions to persist. Exceptional drought conditions continued across southern and southwest Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought was removed from the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough was moving through the western United States with a 175 knot jet rounding the base of it along the International Border.  A surface low with a strong cold front pushed through the Borderland during the morning hours and brought wind gusts up to 93 mph to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 93 mph was recorded at San Augustin Pass. Other gusts across the zone included 88 mph at Salinas Peak, 69 mph at the White Sands Missile Range Main Post, 66 mph at the Missile Range Apache location and 63 mph at Delta Clipper.

Numerous locations along and east of the central recorded peak wind gusts of 58 to 75 mph. Localized dust source regions were activated causing near zero visibility between Roswell and Artesia. This forced the closure of that stretch of U.S. Highway 285. Thankfully there were not reports of accidents. Heavy snow began over the northern mountains on March 24th, continuing over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and adjacent highlands through March 25th and into the early predawn hours of March 26th. Snow totals for the event were very impressive over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains in particular where the Taos and Santa Fe Ski resorts reported 40 and 34 inches respectively. Snow and blowing snow was reported along the U.S. 64 corridor in far northeastern NM between Raton and Clayton during the morning of March 25th resulting in severe winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station near Buck Mountain measured a peak wind gust of 73 mph. High sustained winds peaking to 43 mph were also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level winter storm system had dove down over AZ from March 14th through the 15th, producing a combination of winter weather through the high terrain and high winds through the gaps of the central mountain chain. A relative break in the winter weather arrived during the night of the 15th into the morning of the 16th before winter weather returned to portions of western, central and northern NM. Heavy snow and winter travel impacts were reported over the high terrain. Some areas saw 10 to 18 inches of snow during this second wave of winter weather. In particular, there were reports of near 30 inches of snow for the two winter events combined over a 4 day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Navajo Whiskey SNOTEL observed an estimated 10 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A digging trough across the western US and a potent jet streak rounding its base extended into the southern Great Plains. This strong southwesterly jet streak resulted in widespread high winds across portions of southeast New Mexico and west Texas.

An upper-level disturbance over the Four Corners worked together with a backdoor surface cold front draped across central New Mexico to produce scattered to numerous snow showers across the region on March 8th. Accumulating snowfall with periods of 1 to 2 inch hourly snowfall rates were observed across the northern mountains and central mountain chain. This resulted in some minor traffic accidents. Ski sites across the southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains observed 10 to 15 inches of snowfall. Additionally, 9 to 14 inches of snowfall was observed across the peaks of the Jemez Mountains at Pajarito Ski Resort and just south of Valles Caldera National Preserve. Finally, a couple 7 to 8 inch reports were observed in the Sandia Mountains. Snow showers tapered off during the overnight hours of March 8th into March 9th as overall lift decreased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer at Sandia Park observed 8.3 inches of snowfall. Two more snowfall reports of 7 and 7.5 inches were observed in the zone.

An upper low stalled over Arizona Thursday, March 14th. Snow fell across far northeast New Mexico behind a backdoor cold front during the day. The cutoff upper low drew the backdoor front south and west through the gaps of the central mountain chain with snow filling in its wake that evening. High winds were observed at Albuquerque and the White Sands Missile Range for most of the day on the 15th. Peak gusts reached 60 to 70 mph immediately below the Tijeras Canyon along I-40 and to the Albuquerque Sunport, while also reaching 70 mph near the Oscura Mountains. The high winds caused damage to some parts of Albuquerque with roofs damaged and a recycling truck blown over. Thankfully, there were no fatalities or injuries reported. Meanwhile, significant snow resulted across the northern mountains Thursday night. A band of snow developed over western NM March 15th along the edge of the cold front, resulting in significant snow accumulations through the western and northern mountains Friday night into early Saturday morning, March 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rice Park SNOTEL site observed an estimated snowfall accumulation of 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system crossed northern NM on March 24th with it's associated jet streak crossing much of southeastern NM. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low stalled over Arizona Thursday, March 14th. Snow fell across far northeast New Mexico behind a backdoor cold front during the day. The cutoff upper low drew the backdoor front south and west through the gaps of the central mountain chain with snow filling in its wake that evening. High winds were observed at Albuquerque and the White Sands Missile Range for most of the day on the 15th. Peak gusts reached 60 to 70 mph immediately below the Tijeras Canyon along I-40 and to the Albuquerque Sunport, while also reaching 70 mph near the Oscura Mountains. The high winds caused damage to some parts of Albuquerque with roofs damaged and a recycling truck blown over. Thankfully, there were no fatalities or injuries reported. Meanwhile, significant snow resulted across the northern mountains Thursday night. A band of snow developed over western NM March 15th along the edge of the cold front, resulting in significant snow accumulations through the western and northern mountains Friday night into early Saturday morning, March 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Holman measured 12 inches of snowfall. Angel Fire ski resort observed 10 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system from the Pacific crossed west to east across northern NM on February 2nd bringing heavy snow to the northern mountains of NM. Snow began in western areas of NM during the morning of the 2nd, expanding to the central mountain chain a few hours later. While the Chuska, Jemez, and Tusas Mountains saw accumulations of 6 to 14 inches, heavy snow piled up over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains totaling over 20 inches on some peaks. Taos Ski Valley reported 29 inches by the afternoon of February 3rd. This heavy snow caused severe winter driving conditions over portions of the northern mountains. U.S. Highway 64 between Tierra Amarilla and Tres Piedras was closed due to the winter weather. These severe winter driving conditions spread down the Raton Pass and Johnson/Bartlett Mesas and along U.S. Highway 64 between Raton and Capulin. A strong portion of the jetstream also accompanied this winter system and strengthened over NM as the center of the storm system exited into Texas on February 3rd. Atmospheric mixing and mountain wave activity mainly along and east of the central mountain chain acted to translate these high winds aloft to the surface. High wind gusts of 58 to 75 mph were observed over mountain ranges mainly south of I-40 and over portions of the east-central and southeastern plains of NM. These included two peak wind gusts of 75 mph, one being at the Magdalena Observatory in Catron County and the other by a private weather station northwest of Roswell. The second of which was coincident with a report of a semi rollover 20 miles north of Roswell along U.S. Highway 285. Thankfully, there was no report of injuries or fatalities. Blowing dust was also observed by the Roswell Industrial Airport ASOS causing visibility to fall as low as one statute mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station near Stanley measured high sustained winds peaking at 41 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening closed upper low winter storm system swung across northern and central NM, bringing heavy snow to parts of northern NM on February 10th. A brief period of near winter storm criteria heavy snow also impacted the Albuquerque metro area for a 1 to 2 hour period on the morning of the 10th. This system continued to track over eastern NM, slowing in speed and combining with a backdoor surface cold front pushing southward through eastern NM. These two features combined to produce heavy snow over parts of the northeastern plains during the predawn hours of February 11th, and later past sunrise in the morning through east-central NM from Clovis to Roswell. There was a west to east oriented mesoscale oriented band of heavy snow stretching from near Vaughn to Clovis that dumped 4 to 7 inches of snow in a span of a couple of hours. Heavy snow in Roswell also caused 21 cars to become stuck in snow, needing to be pulled out by the Roswell Community Disaster Relief service. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or fatalities. General snow amounts most areas ranged from 2 to 6 inches with locally higher amounts of near 10 inches atop the peaks of the southern Sangre de Cristo and Jemez Mountains, and a storm total high of 19.5 inches atop Sierra Blanca Peak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pajarito ski area measured 11 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February saw above normal precipitation on the whole, notably from a series of winter storms that passed over the state during the first half of the month. Late month weaker systems brought additional precipitation mainly to the northern mountains of New Mexico. Overall, the above normal precipitation yielded an overall improvement to drought conditions. Extreme drought was removed from the northwest corner of the state with severe drought being reduced in size as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions persisted across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong segment of the jet stream associated with an upper level system dove down across northern and central NM February 26th and 27th. The leading edge of this system brought sporadic high wind gusts to areas of the northern mountains and eastern plains on the 26th before the main segment brought widespread high wind impacts to nearly all areas of northern and central NM. Only far northwestern NM missed out on any high wind impacts. Peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common throughout central and eastern NM on the 27th. Several vehicle crashes coincided with these high gusts, including a semi rollover on I-25 just south of the Albuquerque Sunport, and several crashes from Roswell to Dexter and Hagerman. Near zero visibility from severe blowing dust and brownout conditions was reported at the same time as these accidents in Chaves County between Roswell and Hagerman. One such multi-vehicle crash unfortunately involved a fatality and several injuries. Three Dust Storm Warnings were issued over Chaves County in response to the blowing dust. Winds finally subsided by the late afternoon and early evening of the 27th.||Key Impacts: High winds, dust storm, snow, winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station just north of Portales measured a peak gust of 58 mph.

These severe winter driving conditions spread down the Raton Pass and Johnson/Bartlett Mesas and along U.S. Highway 64 between Raton and Capulin. A strong portion of the jetstream also accompanied this winter system and strengthened over NM as the center of the storm system exited into Texas on February 3rd. Atmospheric mixing and mountain wave activity mainly along and east of the central mountain chain acted to translate these high winds aloft to the surface. High wind gusts of 58 to 75 mph were observed over mountain ranges mainly south of I-40 and over portions of the east-central and southeastern plains of NM. These included two peak wind gusts of 75 mph, one being at the Magdalena Observatory in Catron County and the other by a private weather station northwest of Roswell. The second of which was coincident with a report of a semi rollover 20 miles north of Roswell along U.S. Highway 285. Thankfully, there was no report of injuries or fatalities. Blowing dust was also observed by the Roswell Industrial Airport ASOS causing visibility to fall as low as one statute mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Burro mesonet within the White Sands Missile Range measured high sustained winds peaking at 48 mph, alongside a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was moving onto the Colorado Plains with a 160 knot jet moving across southern New Mexico. Gusty west to northwest winds occurred over the McGregor Range during the late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The McGregor Range RAWS recorded a peak gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening closed upper low winter storm system swung across northern and central NM, bringing heavy snow to parts of northern NM on February 10th. A brief period of near winter storm criteria heavy snow also impacted the Albuquerque metro area for a 1 to 2 hour period on the morning of the 10th. This system continued to track over eastern NM, slowing in speed and combining with a backdoor surface cold front pushing southward through eastern NM. These two features combined to produce heavy snow over parts of the northeastern plains during the predawn hours of February 11th, and later past sunrise in the morning through east-central NM from Clovis to Roswell. There was a west to east oriented mesoscale oriented band of heavy snow stretching from near Vaughn to Clovis that dumped 4 to 7 inches of snow in a span of a couple of hours. Heavy snow in Roswell also caused 21 cars to become stuck in snow, needing to be pulled out by the Roswell Community Disaster Relief service. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or fatalities. General snow amounts most areas ranged from 2 to 6 inches with locally higher amounts of near 10 inches atop the peaks of the southern Sangre de Cristo and Jemez Mountains, and a storm total high of 19.5 inches atop Sierra Blanca Peak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS station near of Alto measured 19.5 inches of snow. General amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches in populated areas including Ruidoso, and 10 to 17 inches in the higher terrain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February saw above normal precipitation on the whole, notably from a series of winter storms that passed over the state during the first half of the month. Late month weaker systems brought additional precipitation mainly to the northern mountains of New Mexico. Overall, the above normal precipitation yielded an overall improvement to drought conditions. Extreme drought was removed from the northwest corner of the state with severe drought being reduced in size as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought persisted across the southern quarter of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong segment of the jet stream associated with an upper level system dove down across northern and central NM February 26th and 27th. The leading edge of this system brought sporadic high wind gusts to areas of the northern mountains and eastern plains on the 26th before the main segment brought widespread high wind impacts to nearly all areas of northern and central NM. Only far northwestern NM missed out on any high wind impacts. Peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common throughout central and eastern NM on the 27th. Several vehicle crashes coincided with these high gusts, including a semi rollover on I-25 just south of the Albuquerque Sunport, and several crashes from Roswell to Dexter and Hagerman. Near zero visibility from severe blowing dust and brownout conditions was reported at the same time as these accidents in Chaves County between Roswell and Hagerman. One such multi-vehicle crash unfortunately involved a fatality and several injuries. Three Dust Storm Warnings were issued over Chaves County in response to the blowing dust. Winds finally subsided by the late afternoon and early evening of the 27th.||Key Impacts: High winds, dust storm, snow, winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from the Dexter Fire Chief, Chaves County Emergency Manager and County Sheriff showed multiple multi-vehicle crashes between Roswell and Hagerman during this time. The relief route between West McGaffey Street and Sunset Place on the southwest side of Roswell was closed due to near zero visibility at 10:25 MST. A two vehicle crash was reported at 13:35 at Shuswap Road and Old Dexter Highway between Dexter and Hagerman from near zero visibility in blowing dust. A multi-vehicle crash involving seven vehicles was reported at Orchard Park Road and U.S. Highway 285 at 14:30 MST from low visibility in blowing dust. Additional cars became involved by 14:40 MST. Two passengers were trapped in a vehicle fire with one fatality. Responders reported they did not see the vehicle fire initially due to the brownout conditions. At 15:30 MST, another crash involving three vehicles was reported at Wilshire Boulevard and Main Street in Roswell from low visibility in blowing dust. The emergency manager also reported Roswell schools closed 12:00 MST.

Taos Ski Valley reported 29 inches by the afternoon of February 3rd. This heavy snow caused severe winter driving conditions over portions of the northern mountains. Atmospheric mixing and mountain wave activity mainly along and east of the central mountain chain acted to translate these high winds aloft to the surface. High wind gusts of 58 to 75 mph were observed over mountain ranges mainly south of I-40 and over portions of the east-central and southeastern plains of NM. These included two peak wind gusts of 75 mph, one being at the Magdalena Observatory in Catron County and the other by a private weather station northwest of Roswell. The second of which was coincident with a report of a semi rollover 20 miles north of Roswell along U.S. Highway 285. Thankfully, there was no report of injuries or fatalities. Blowing dust was also observed by the Roswell Industrial Airport ASOS causing visibility to fall as low as one statute mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nogal NMCC measured a peak wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low moved across the Four Corners region and across northern New Mexico.  An associated cold front helped to bring upslope snow to the Cloudcroft area where up to a foot of snow was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 6 to 12 inches of snow fell within a couple miles of Cloudcroft. Twelve inches was reported 1.8 miles southwest of Cloudcroft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough across much of the United States combined with a 150 knot jet over New Mexico brought a very windy day to the Borderland with a peak gust of 93 mph reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 93 mph was reported at San Augustin Pass.  Other gusts across the zone included 91 mph at Salinas Peak, 78 mph at the White Sands Main Post and 66 mph at Condron Field on the White Sands Missile Range.

The leading edge of this system brought sporadic high wind gusts to areas of the northern mountains and eastern plains on the 26th before the main segment brought widespread high wind impacts to nearly all areas of northern and central NM. Only far northwestern NM missed out on any high wind impacts. Peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph were common throughout central and eastern NM on the 27th. Several vehicle crashes coincided with these high gusts, including a semi rollover on I-25 just south of the Albuquerque Sunport, and several crashes from Roswell to Dexter and Hagerman. Near zero visibility from severe blowing dust and brownout conditions was reported at the same time as these accidents in Chaves County between Roswell and Hagerman. One such multi-vehicle crash unfortunately involved a fatality and several injuries. Three Dust Storm Warnings were issued over Chaves County in response to the blowing dust. Winds finally subsided by the late afternoon and early evening of the 27th.||Key Impacts: High winds, dust storm, snow, winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Magdalena Ridge Observatory measured gusts of 60 to 70 mph throughout this time frame with a peak gust of 81 mph at 12:34pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system from the Pacific crossed west to east across northern NM on February 2nd bringing heavy snow to the northern mountains of NM. Snow began in western areas of NM during the morning of the 2nd, expanding to the central mountain chain a few hours later. While the Chuska, Jemez, and Tusas Mountains saw accumulations of 6 to 14 inches, heavy snow piled up over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains totaling over 20 inches on some peaks. Taos Ski Valley reported 29 inches by the afternoon of February 3rd. This heavy snow caused severe winter driving conditions over portions of the northern mountains. U.S. Highway 64 between Tierra Amarilla and Tres Piedras was closed due to the winter weather. These severe winter driving conditions spread down the Raton Pass and Johnson/Bartlett Mesas and along U.S. Highway 64 between Raton and Capulin. A strong portion of the jetstream also accompanied this winter system and strengthened over NM as the center of the storm system exited into Texas on February 3rd. Atmospheric mixing and mountain wave activity mainly along and east of the central mountain chain acted to translate these high winds aloft to the surface. High wind gusts of 58 to 75 mph were observed over mountain ranges mainly south of I-40 and over portions of the east-central and southeastern plains of NM. These included two peak wind gusts of 75 mph, one being at the Magdalena Observatory in Catron County and the other by a private weather station northwest of Roswell. The second of which was coincident with a report of a semi rollover 20 miles north of Roswell along U.S. Highway 285. Thankfully, there was no report of injuries or fatalities. Blowing dust was also observed by the Roswell Industrial Airport ASOS causing visibility to fall as low as one statute mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roswell Airport ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of concurrent winter storms rolled across northern and central NM from late February 7th through the morning of February 8th. Strong southwesterly winds ahead of this storm system rolled through much of the central highlands and eastern plains of New Mexico on February 7th. Two mesonet stations in western Guadalupe County measured peak wind gusts passing 60 mph. Isolated heavy snow was also observed over the peaks of the western and northern mountains. Snow levels were too high to allow for winter related impacts to reach down to more populated valley areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton Airport ASOS measured a peak gusts of 67 mph. Wind gusts regularly surpassed 58 mph during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large trough along the Rocky Mountains with a southwesterly jet streak rounding its base brought southwesterly high winds to the Guadalupe Mountains and southeast New Mexico.

A negatively tilted shortwave trough over the central Rocky Mountains brought an early-season flux of moisture into the Permian Basin. As the surface cyclone developed and moved east, a strong Pacific front brought strong westerly winds to portions of the area. A localized thunderstorm wind gust was also observed with convection along the advancing front.

A negatively tilted trough over the southern Great Plains and an associated westerly jet streak rounding its base brought westerly high winds to a large portion of southeast New Mexico and west Texas.

A strengthening closed upper low winter storm system swung across northern and central NM, bringing heavy snow to parts of northern NM on February 10th. A brief period of near winter storm criteria heavy snow also impacted the Albuquerque metro area for a 1 to 2 hour period on the morning of the 10th. This system continued to track over eastern NM, slowing in speed and combining with a backdoor surface cold front pushing southward through eastern NM. These two features combined to produce heavy snow over parts of the northeastern plains during the predawn hours of February 11th, and later past sunrise in the morning through east-central NM from Clovis to Roswell. There was a west to east oriented mesoscale oriented band of heavy snow stretching from near Vaughn to Clovis that dumped 4 to 7 inches of snow in a span of a couple of hours. Heavy snow in Roswell also caused 21 cars to become stuck in snow, needing to be pulled out by the Roswell Community Disaster Relief service. Thankfully, there were no reports of injuries or fatalities. General snow amounts most areas ranged from 2 to 6 inches with locally higher amounts of near 10 inches atop the peaks of the southern Sangre de Cristo and Jemez Mountains, and a storm total high of 19.5 inches atop Sierra Blanca Peak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roswell Community Disaster Relief pulled out 21 cars that were stuck in snow. Reports across Roswell ranged from 2 to 5 inches of snow total. There were no reports of injury or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening upper-level disturbance moved through the Desert Southwest and Four Corners on February 21st with a Pacific cold front sweeping through New Mexico at the surface. High wind impacts occurred at the Sierra Blanca airport in the Sacramento Mountains with a high sustained wind of 43 mph and wind gust of 59 mph. A 67 mph wind gust was observed at the Roswell airport in the Chaves County Plains, and 58 and 69 mph wind gusts at Dunken in southwest Chaves County. The 67 mph wind gust at Roswell came with haze as a visibility as low as 1.25 miles. Strong southwest and west winds tapered off gradually after sunset.||An upper level disturbance crossed the Desert Southwest February 21st with an associated surface cold front. Deep layer mixing allowed for strong winds aloft to reach most of northern and central NM during the afternoon period. High winds were observed at several locations over south-central and eastern NM including the Sierra Blanca Airport near Ruidoso, Roswell, and Dunken. Numerous dust sources, including the White Sands National Monument became activated blowing dust and sand across the Sacramento Mountains and over Chaves County. Low visibility falling to one and a quarter mile was observed at the Roswell Airport. Thankfully no impacts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sierra Blanca Airport recorded a peak wind gust of 59 mph. Sustained winds also reached a high of 43 mph. Blowing sand from Wind Sands National Park blew over the south-central mountains as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system from the Pacific crossed west to east across northern NM on February 2nd bringing heavy snow to the northern mountains of NM. Snow began in western areas of NM during the morning of the 2nd, expanding to the central mountain chain a few hours later. While the Chuska, Jemez, and Tusas Mountains saw accumulations of 6 to 14 inches, heavy snow piled up over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains totaling over 20 inches on some peaks. Taos Ski Valley reported 29 inches by the afternoon of February 3rd. This heavy snow caused severe winter driving conditions over portions of the northern mountains. U.S. Highway 64 between Tierra Amarilla and Tres Piedras was closed due to the winter weather. These severe winter driving conditions spread down the Raton Pass and Johnson/Bartlett Mesas and along U.S. Highway 64 between Raton and Capulin. Atmospheric mixing and mountain wave activity mainly along and east of the central mountain chain acted to translate these high winds aloft to the surface. These included two peak wind gusts of 75 mph, one being at the Magdalena Observatory in Catron County and the other by a private weather station northwest of Roswell. The second of which was coincident with a report of a semi rollover 20 miles north of Roswell along U.S. Highway 285. Thankfully, there was no report of injuries or fatalities. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taos Ski Valley area reported amounts ranging from 27 to 33 inches of snow within their area. Other reports across the zone ranged from 14 to 22 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon severe thunderstorms would develop across eastern New Mexico and merge into an MCS that would track through portions of southeast New Mexico and the Permian Basin late in the evening. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reports water standing on all roads near Tatum, including Highways 206 and 380.

A positively tilted shortwave trough lifting out of the Four Corners and a stalled cold front over the Permian Basin aided in afternoon thunderstorm development. Numerous thunderstorms formed along and north of the cold front and became an intense squall line with damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Return flow developed during the day on November 1st increasing surface moisture across southeast NM. Lift ahead of a weak upper level disturbance resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms after sunset November 1st continuing into the morning hours of November 2nd. Storms ended up moving from southwest to northeast over the same areas across Chaves and Roosevelt County resulting in flash flooding across southern and eastern Chaves County and in northern Roosevelt County west of Portales. Another round of storms developed across southeast NM around midday November 2nd before exiting east into West Texas during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video shows the water flowing over Bottomless Lakes Road north of Bottomless Lakes State Park. The road was closed due to being impassible from the floodwaters. Additionally, water was flowing across U.S. Highway 380 at mile marker 177 and at a rest stop next to the Mescalero Sands North Dune area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported bar ditches full of water from heavy rainfall, and State Highway 267 (Floyd Highway) had several places covered in 6 to 12 inches of water. However, the road still remained passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall, October was record warm month for northern and central NM with dry conditions during the first half of the month. The 2nd half of the month was much wetter, especially for eastern New Mexico with Roswell observing record rainfall for a 24-hour period on October 19th resulting in catastrophic flooding across the city. This rainfall resulted in the removal of severe drought across Roswell and southern Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions were mostly removed from the southern half of the zone due to a record amount of rainfall observed on October 19th. Roswell observed 5.78 inches of rain on October 19th setting a new all-time record for daily rainfall in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Return flow developed during the day on November 1st increasing surface moisture across southeast NM. Lift ahead of a weak upper level disturbance resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms after sunset November 1st continuing into the morning hours of November 2nd. Storms ended up moving from southwest to northeast over the same areas across Chaves and Roosevelt County resulting in flash flooding across southern and eastern Chaves County and in northern Roosevelt County west of Portales. Another round of storms developed across southeast NM around midday November 2nd before exiting east into West Texas during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lots of hail up to quarter size covering the ground in Melrose.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front backed through eastern NM and westward through the gaps of the central mountain chain, including the Tijeras Canyon along I-40 in eastern Albuquerque. Strong winds funneling through this canyon saw peak gusts reach sub-severe levels of 40 to 47 mph. It was strong enough however to knock over a large tree in the South Valley area of the Albuquerque metro area overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong east canyon winds knocked down a full grown large tree in the South Valley area of Albuquerque at La Vega Court SW and Riverside Drive SW. Time was estimated to be between 1am and 2am MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season upper level low brought winter weather across the higher elevations of the northern and central NM November 4th. While snow levels fell to around 7,000 feet, significant accumulating snow was limited to portions of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and Raton Pass area. Closures of I-25 at Raton Pass and a few power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rio Barbara SNOTEL recorded an estimated 11 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough aided in the development of severe thunderstorms along the higher terrain of the Sacramento and Guadalupe Mountains.
Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along the higher terrain of southeast New Mexico. A few thunderstorms produced locally damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported that four power poles were snapped off by thunderstorm wind gusts. Wind gust speed and property damage cost are estimations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, convectively enhanced upper-low over the Texas/New Mexico border aided in the development of severe thunderstorms ahead of this feature. This included hail, damaging winds, and a couple of brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter shared a photo of half inch to one inch hail covering the ground. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, convectively enhanced upper-low over the Texas/New Mexico border aided in the development of severe thunderstorms ahead of this feature. This included hail, damaging winds, and a couple of brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported ping pong ball size hail. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front behind an upper level disturbance over the upper Midwest moved into far northeast NM the morning of July 7th. Additionally, a remnant outflow boundary was draped across far east central NM and west Texas. These two surface features combined with higher moisture resulted in the development of strong to severe storms across these areas during the mid afternoon and evening hours. Another round of storms moved across northeast and east central NM overnight as the backdoor front surged west through the gaps of the central mountain chain. Storms caused some isolated flooding across Cannon Air Force Base. Showers and storms exited into the Texas Panhandle during the mid morning hours on July 8th. Another round of strong to severe storms developed over the north central mountains midday July 8th with penny to nickel size hail observed in the Jemez Mountains and quarter size hail near Ute Park. Some flash flooding was also observed in Las Vegas. Storms dissipated over central New Mexico shortly after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer near Fenton Lake State Park in the Jemez Mountains observed penny size hail from a strong thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoon high over northern Arizona on July 19th continued backing west to over the Great Basin July 20th and 21st in response to upper level troughing over the central U.S. The upper level trough over the central U.S. helped bring a backdoor front through eastern NM early on July 20th. In between the two synoptic features, upper level disturbances travelled south through New Mexico. This resulted in a greater coverage of afternoon and evening showers and storms over the region both days with activity slowly waning overnight. Storms over the South Fork burn scar brought another round of flash flooding to the Ruidoso area Saturday afternoon with the Rio Ruidoso rising to a record level of 15.39 feet July 20th. Flash flooding was reported from storms in Portales mid Sunday afternoon. Another round of flash flooding occurred in Ruidoso Sunday night from storms moving south through the area. Daytime heating on Monday resulted in another crop of showers and storms mainly across the higher terrain Monday afternoon and evening moving south with time. Storms produced some flash flooding on the northeast part of the Hermits Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar and a peak wind gust of 62 mph on the White Sands Missile Range near Oscuro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms produced a 58 mph wind gust at the Albuquerque Sunport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active monsoon pattern across New Mexico resulted in slight drought improvement across the state. 1 category improvements were observed across south central and eastern New Mexico. Several instances of flash flooding were observed across Ruidoso due to heavy rainfall on the South Fork and Salt Fire burn scars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought remained across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough moving across the Great Plains and an advancing front helped to initiate a band of thunderstorms that moved across southeast New Mexico and portions of the Permian Basin. Localized damaging wind gusts and a landspout were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: LANDSPOUT. Location: 32,400, -104.260/32.400, 104.250. Public report of a brief landspout near Carlsbad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level high pressure remained over southern California and Nevada with higher moisture across central and eastern NM. Strong daytime heating resulted in the development of showers and storms across the higher terrain of central New Mexico around midday July 9th with storms slowly drifting to the southeast. Storms over the Sacramento Mountains dropped 1 to 2 inches of rain on the South Fork and Salt Fire burn scars around Ruidoso. This resulted in catastrophic burn scar flash flooding and debris flows in the Rio Ruidoso, NM48, and U.S. Highway 70 from Alto east to Ruidoso Downs. The runoff and debris flow caused the Rio Ruidoso at Hollywood to quickly spike to 11.65 feet in the mid afternoon. A landspout was also observed in the upper canyon area west of downtown Ruidoso. Neighborhoods west of downtown Ruidoso were evacuated and 64 swift water rescues were conducted. Storms exited east of Ruidoso late in the afternoon with flash flooding subsiding in the evening. Elsewhere in the state, isolated severe storms produced quarter size hail at Chamizal in Socorro County and a mile east of Ute Park in Colfax County. Storms across central and eastern New Mexico dissipated overnight. Another round of storms developed across the central mountain chain midday July 10th. Multiple rounds of training storms over the Sacramento Mountains dropped a half inch to two inches across the Ruidoso area including the South Fork and Salt Fire burn scars. This resulted in a second consecutive day of catastrophic flash flooding, debris flows and road collapses in the same areas of Ruidoso. Several swift water rescues were conducted again along U.S. Highway 70. The Rio Ruidoso at Hollywood spiked to a record level of 12.51 feet from the runoff and debris flow off the burn scars. Storms and high flows in area creeks and streams across Ruidoso slowly decreased during the evening hours. Elsewhere, storms caused high wind gusts at the Little Burro mesonet on the White Sand Missile Range and at the Dunken Mesonet on the White Sands Missile Range. Storms across the state dissipated shortly after sunset. Another round of diurnal storms developed during the afternoon and evening hours on July 11th. Outflow winds from thunderstorms resulted in roof damage to a residence in Little Water in west central San Juan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ruidoso emergency manager forwarded a video of a landspout in the Upper Canyon area. The time, length and width of landspout estimated based on derived radar products and video footage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ridge Wildfire was a lightning caused fire in a remote area of the Gila National Forest in southern Catron County. The fire surpassed 4,000 acres by July 17th before wetter weather slowed its growth. The fire forced closures within the Gila National Forest, mainly along trails.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ridge Wildfire started in the Gila National Forest in southern Catron County reaching a total size of 4,257 acres. Little to no damage was reported outside of closures along trails.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoon high over northern Arizona on July 19th continued backing west to over the Great Basin July 20th and 21st in response to upper level troughing over the central U.S. The upper level trough over the central U.S. helped bring a backdoor front through eastern NM early on July 20th. In between the two synoptic features, upper level disturbances travelled south through New Mexico. This resulted in a greater coverage of afternoon and evening showers and storms over the region both days with activity slowly waning overnight. Storms over the South Fork burn scar brought another round of flash flooding to the Ruidoso area Saturday afternoon with the Rio Ruidoso rising to a record level of 15.39 feet July 20th. Flash flooding was reported from storms in Portales mid Sunday afternoon. Another round of flash flooding occurred in Ruidoso Sunday night from storms moving south through the area. Daytime heating on Monday resulted in another crop of showers and storms mainly across the higher terrain Monday afternoon and evening moving south with time. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorms on the South Fork burn scar resulted in another round of burn scar flash flooding, debris flow and severe property damage across the Upper Canyon and Brady Canyon neighborhoods along Mechem Drive west of downtown Ruidoso. Bridge 2 at Main Road and Fox Road became impassible due to flooding. Debris flows resulted in arching powerlines over the flood water at Brady Canyon. An SUV was trapped against a tree due to flood waters just north of Brady Canyon Road. There was a rescue of two juveniles from a house at Meander Drive and Paradise Canyon Drive. Runoff from the flash flooding resulted in the Rio Ruidoso to instantly rise to major flood stage and a new record level of 15.80 feet. This resulted in more flash flooding at the Ruidoso Downs Casino horse racetrack. Flash flooding caused damage to the racetrack, two bridges near the entrance of the facility, and in the barn area. After this latest instance of flash flooding, officials decided to shut down the horse racetrack for the remainder of the summer season and move upcoming scheduled races to Albuquerque. Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported from this instance of burn scar flash flooding. Property damage is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across the central Rockies during the day increasing upper-level winds across New Mexico. Deep daytime mixing allowed these stronger winds aloft to mix down to the surface resulting in a brief high wind gust at the Clayton Municipal Airport. Winds tapered off in the evening hours around sunset as the upper-level disturbance exited east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief high wind gust of 59 mph was observed at the Clayton Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving across the Great Basin and Central Rockies combined with record levels of monsoon moisture resulted in the development of widespread showers and storms across northern and central NM each and every afternoon from July 1st through July 3rd. These thunderstorms produced high wind gusts of 59 and 68 mph at the Belen Regional Airport and the Mine mesonet station on the White Sands Missile Range respectively on July 1st. There was a 59 mph wind gust at the Raton Crews Airport on July 3rd. Drier air moved in from the northwest on the backside of the disturbance on July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow from approaching thunderstorms produced a 59 mph wind gust at the Belen Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable placement of the monsoon high over NM and then shifting to the west allowed for an influx of moisture and instability over the state of NM. Daily rounds of of showers and storms starting over the higher terrain midday spreading to lower elevation in the evening before slowly dissipating overnight each day. There were several reports of flash flooding over portions of central NM and a few severe thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A late evening batch of moderate to heavy showers passed over the complex of burn scars in and around Ruidoso producing a half inch to one inch of rainfall in the area. Catastrophic flash flooding occurred, focusing through Cedar Creek and the Rio Ruidoso after 11pm MDT. Flood waters overtopping bridges and roadways were reported in several areas including Mechem Dr. at Brady Canyon Dr., U.S. Highway 70 at Cedar Creek, and Paradise Canyon Road at Cedar Creek. Evacuations were ordered from the Upper Canyon area to areas along the Rio Ruidoso from west of town through to mobile home areas near Hollywood along U.S. Highway 70. Several swift water rescues were conducted, including for two vehicles trapped in water near Johnson Rd., an elderly resident who was unable to evacuate on Brady Canyon Dr., and one resident trapped on one side of a bridge at Paradise Rd. and Foothill Rd. that was overtopped by water. Cherokee Mobile Home Park sustained damage with water flowing beneath mobile homes. Home damage was reported along Brady Canyon Dr. from the elderly couple who needed assistance evacuating. A culvert on Cedar Creek above the ranger station was completely torn out and washed out onto the roadway. Rises on the Rio Ruidoso at the Hollywood gage spiked from 1.58 feet to 8.16 feet within 90 minutes before waters started to recede.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front entered northeast NM during the late afternoon hours of July 4th. Higher moisture behind this front resulted in the development of a severe storm near the Colorado border that dropped ping pong ball size in Raton during the evening. The backdoor front surged through eastern NM overnight July 4th into the morning hours of July 5th producing more showers and storms in its wake. The backdoor front was draped across the central mountain chain on July 5th with more storms developing along the east slopes of the central mountain chain come midday. Storms produced quarter to golf ball size hail in Mora, San Miguel, Guadalupe, and Lincoln Counties along with a brief tornado a few miles east southeast of Sapello in San Miguel County, and a high wind gust of 65 mph at Dunken in southwest Chaves County. Storms moved into the eastern plains during the evening followed with another round of storms across northeast NM during the overnight hours of July 5th. This round of storms slowly dissipated near the Texas border come daybreak on July 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped ping pong size hail a few miles south of Raton along Interstate 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level high pressure remained over southern California and Nevada with higher moisture across central and eastern NM. Strong daytime heating resulted in the development of showers and storms across the higher terrain of central New Mexico around midday July 9th with storms slowly drifting to the southeast. Storms over the Sacramento Mountains dropped 1 to 2 inches of rain on the South Fork and Salt Fire burn scars around Ruidoso. This resulted in catastrophic burn scar flash flooding and debris flows in the Rio Ruidoso, NM48, and U.S. Highway 70 from Alto east to Ruidoso Downs. The runoff and debris flow caused the Rio Ruidoso at Hollywood to quickly spike to 11.65 feet in the mid afternoon. A landspout was also observed in the upper canyon area west of downtown Ruidoso. Neighborhoods west of downtown Ruidoso were evacuated and 64 swift water rescues were conducted. Storms exited east of Ruidoso late in the afternoon with flash flooding subsiding in the evening. Elsewhere in the state, isolated severe storms produced quarter size hail at Chamizal in Socorro County and a mile east of Ute Park in Colfax County. Storms across central and eastern New Mexico dissipated overnight. Another round of storms developed across the central mountain chain midday July 10th. Multiple rounds of training storms over the Sacramento Mountains dropped a half inch to two inches across the Ruidoso area including the South Fork and Salt Fire burn scars. This resulted in a second consecutive day of catastrophic flash flooding, debris flows and road collapses in the same areas of Ruidoso. Several swift water rescues were conducted again along U.S. Highway 70. The Rio Ruidoso at Hollywood spiked to a record level of 12.51 feet from the runoff and debris flow off the burn scars. Storms and high flows in area creeks and streams across Ruidoso slowly decreased during the evening hours. Elsewhere, storms caused high wind gusts at the Little Burro mesonet on the White Sand Missile Range and at the Dunken Mesonet on the White Sands Missile Range. Storms across the state dissipated shortly after sunset. Another round of diurnal storms developed during the afternoon and evening hours on July 11th. Outflow winds from thunderstorms resulted in roof damage to a residence in Little Water in west central San Juan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms on the South Fork and Salt Fire burn scars resulted in flash flooding and debris flows across the Upper Canyon and Brady Canyon neighborhoods along the Rio Ruidoso. A bridge on Main Road at McDaniel Road was washed from the debris flow and runoff. Video on social media showed the roof of a house along with other household debris sliding down the Rio Ruidoso upper canyon among fast moving water. Additionally, a woman was trapped in a tree due to high rushing water. Runoff from the rainfall moved downstream through the Rio Ruidoso exceeding its banks and impacting the Fern Trail and Paradise Canyon near downtown Ruidoso. Several car and water rescues were conducted in this area. The Riverside RV Park in Hollywood along the banks of the Rio Ruidoso was completely flooded out. Another video on social media showed the Rio Ruidoso at Ruidoso Downs Racetrack and Casino out of its banks flooding the horse racetrack. Additionally, crews with excavators are seen digging up dirt along the banks of the Rio Ruidoso at the racetrack cleaning up prior flash flood damage. Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported from the catastrophic flash flooding. Property damage cost is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northern areas of the forecast area saw improving drought conditions with a large area ending the month of July with no drought or Abnormally Dry(D0) drought conditions.  Areas across the south generally remained unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started with Extreme(D3) drought over the south and improving to Moderate(D1) drought across the north.  By the end of the month, the far northern areas had improved to Abnormally Dry(D0) drought with little change to the south. The White Sands National Park Cooperative Observer reported 0.89 inches of precipitation which was 0.75 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level high pressure remained over southern California and Nevada with higher moisture across central and eastern NM. Strong daytime heating resulted in the development of showers and storms across the higher terrain of central New Mexico around midday July 9th with storms slowly drifting to the southeast. Storms over the Sacramento Mountains dropped 1 to 2 inches of rain on the South Fork and Salt Fire burn scars around Ruidoso. This resulted in catastrophic burn scar flash flooding and debris flows in the Rio Ruidoso, NM48, and U.S. Highway 70 from Alto east to Ruidoso Downs. The runoff and debris flow caused the Rio Ruidoso at Hollywood to quickly spike to 11.65 feet in the mid afternoon. A landspout was also observed in the upper canyon area west of downtown Ruidoso. Neighborhoods west of downtown Ruidoso were evacuated and 64 swift water rescues were conducted. Storms exited east of Ruidoso late in the afternoon with flash flooding subsiding in the evening. Elsewhere in the state, isolated severe storms produced quarter size hail at Chamizal in Socorro County and a mile east of Ute Park in Colfax County. Storms across central and eastern New Mexico dissipated overnight. Another round of storms developed across the central mountain chain midday July 10th. Multiple rounds of training storms over the Sacramento Mountains dropped a half inch to two inches across the Ruidoso area including the South Fork and Salt Fire burn scars. This resulted in a second consecutive day of catastrophic flash flooding, debris flows and road collapses in the same areas of Ruidoso. Several swift water rescues were conducted again along U.S. Highway 70. The Rio Ruidoso at Hollywood spiked to a record level of 12.51 feet from the runoff and debris flow off the burn scars. Storms and high flows in area creeks and streams across Ruidoso slowly decreased during the evening hours. Elsewhere, storms caused high wind gusts at the Little Burro mesonet on the White Sand Missile Range and at the Dunken Mesonet on the White Sands Missile Range. Storms across the state dissipated shortly after sunset. Another round of diurnal storms developed during the afternoon and evening hours on July 11th. Outflow winds from thunderstorms resulted in roof damage to a residence in Little Water in west central San Juan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of heavy rainfall from thunderstorms impacted the South Fork and Salt Fire burn scars for a second day in a row. This resulted in exacerbated flash flooding and debris flow in the same locations along the Rio Ruidoso. A house near downtown Ruidoso was reported to have been pushed 10 feet due to flash flooding. A busted water line and gas leak were also reported in the area. An SUV with 3 occupants inside floated down the Rio Ruidoso. Thankfully, all 3 occupants were rescued. The worst impacts from this episode of flash flooding and debris flow occurred on U.S. Highway 70 in Hollywood. The highway and its various intersections, including Mechem Drive and Sudderth Drive were shut down. Fast and heavy debris flow resulted in a road collapse near the Sudderth Dr. and U.S. Highway 70 intersection. The debris flowing down U.S. Highway 70 at one point included a large shipping container. Property damage cost is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoon high slid east of NM over TX bringing monsoon moisture northward into NM. Slow steering flow coupled with moisture allowed for two more days of burn scar flash flooding on the complex of burn scars in and around Ruidoso. The first of which caused only a minor road closure near Mechem Drive. The second of which was far more widespread bringing impacts from Alto to Ruidoso to Ruidoso Downs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported Brady Canyon Dr. and Ponderosa Dr. were closed due to water flowing over the roadway, caused by burn scar flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough existed near the Continental Divide while a broad upper trough was moving through the Great Basin.  Deeper moisture existed west of the Divide and helped fuel storms over the Gila region where a wind gust to 60 mph was recorded in Silver City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a 60 mph wind gust along with a lot of pea size hail and a half inch of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was located over the Great Basin with northeast aloft across southern New Mexico.  At the surface, dry air existed with dew point depressions around 50 degrees.  A few thunderstorms moved southwest across the Lordsburg Playa that produced wind gusts to over 60 mph and near zero visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Mesonet station on the Lordsburg Playa near mile marker 7 reported a peak gust of 67 mph. Several other stations on the playa reported gusts of 60 to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tanques Wildfire was a lightning started fire within the Santa Fe National Forest beginning July 18th, located 27 miles north of Cuba, NM. The fire became a managed wildfire reaching 5,800 acres by July 26th. The fire burned in remote wilderness and caused no damage to any structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning start wildfire 27 miles north of Cuba that was managed to 5,800 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front behind an upper level disturbance over the upper Midwest moved into far northeast NM the morning of July 7th. Additionally, a remnant outflow boundary was draped across far east central NM and west Texas. These two surface features combined with higher moisture resulted in the development of strong to severe storms across these areas during the mid afternoon and evening hours. Another round of storms moved across northeast and east central NM overnight as the backdoor front surged west through the gaps of the central mountain chain. Storms caused some isolated flooding across Cannon Air Force Base. Showers and storms exited into the Texas Panhandle during the mid morning hours on July 8th. Another round of strong to severe storms developed over the north central mountains midday July 8th with penny to nickel size hail observed in the Jemez Mountains and quarter size hail near Ute Park. Some flash flooding was also observed in Las Vegas. Storms dissipated over central New Mexico shortly after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter a few miles near Ranchville reported half inch to penny size hail from a brief strong thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoon high over northern Arizona on July 19th continued backing west to over the Great Basin July 20th and 21st in response to upper level troughing over the central U.S. The upper level trough over the central U.S. helped bring a backdoor front through eastern NM early on July 20th. In between the two synoptic features, upper level disturbances travelled south through New Mexico. This resulted in a greater coverage of afternoon and evening showers and storms over the region both days with activity slowly waning overnight. Storms over the South Fork burn scar brought another round of flash flooding to the Ruidoso area Saturday afternoon with the Rio Ruidoso rising to a record level of 15.39 feet July 20th. Flash flooding was reported from storms in Portales mid Sunday afternoon. Another round of flash flooding occurred in Ruidoso Sunday night from storms moving south through the area. Daytime heating on Monday resulted in another crop of showers and storms mainly across the higher terrain Monday afternoon and evening moving south with time. Storms produced some flash flooding on the northeast part of the Hermits Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar and a peak wind gust of 62 mph on the White Sands Missile Range near Oscuro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tijeras Arroyo overtopped Via Sedillo for the second time in a week. The large embankment created by road crews to protect the roadway from additional flood waters was blown out again. Mud, rocks, and debris covered a large section of the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoon high slid east of NM over TX bringing monsoon moisture northward into NM. Slow steering flow coupled with moisture allowed for two more days of burn scar flash flooding on the complex of burn scars in and around Ruidoso. The first of which caused only a minor road closure near Mechem Drive. The second of which was far more widespread bringing impacts from Alto to Ruidoso to Ruidoso Downs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 0.50 to 1.50 inches fell on the South Fork burn scar west of downtown Ruidoso during the early afternoon hours. This rainfall resulted in more burn scar flash flooding and debris flows across neighborhoods in Alto and Ruidoso. The Cherokee Mobile Village along U.S. Highway 70 at the Lincoln and Otero County line was evacuated due to flash flooding. Mechem Dr. became impassible again due to Cedar Creek overflowing its banks and rushing over the roadway. Heavy flows along Cedar Creek caused Mechem Dr. to become impassable, washed out a part of Musket Ball Dr. and brought debris flow over Cree Meadows Drive. Flooding of the Rio Ruidoso resulted in the closure of Gavilan Canyon Road and the evacuation of the Gavilan Mobile Home Park. Debris flow on the Rio Ruidoso destroyed a culvert on the eastern end of the Ruidoso Downs racetrack. Water and debris flow reported west of Alto making NM 532 impassable. Fortunately, no reports of significant water rises were received downstream of the water breach at Ruidoso Downs. Culvert and trailer park damage cost is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deeper monsoonal moisture moved into the region with a strong upper high located across northeast Texas. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two outflows from nearby thunderstorms collided over the Chaparral area which created a gustnado as well as straight-line wind damage. There was a complete collapse of a well built outbuilding/garage.  The roof separated from the walls allowing the wind to get inside and under the roof structure.  The roof debris scattered towards the west and northwest and was displaced approximately 50 yards from the foundation. The walls collapsed afterwards with all of the walls falling in a southward direction.  Metal fencing attached to a rock wall was ripped off.  An RV camper rolled on it's side and was damaged by flying debris. There was a metal shed that collapsed as winds lifted the roof from the structure.  Finally, an outdoor shed was completely destroyed with debris scattered up to 100 yards towards the northwest.

Drought conditions remained very consistent across the state as a whole, with only localized changes observed in San Miguel and eastern Chaves Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance was moved across Colorado along with a backdoor front draped across northeast NM. Higher moisture and upslope flow were in place north and east of the front. Daytime heating in the afternoon, along with lift from upper-level disturbance and backdoor front allowed storms to develop in far northeast New Mexico moving to the northeast with time. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance was moved across Colorado along with a backdoor front draped across northeast NM. Higher moisture and upslope flow were in place north and east of the front. Daytime heating in the afternoon, along with lift from upper-level disturbance and backdoor front allowed storms to develop in far northeast New Mexico moving to the northeast with time. Two storms produced quarter size diameter hail in Grenville and Clayton. Storms exited east into the Texas and Oklahoma panhandles during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced penny to quarter sized hail in Clayton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Encerrita Wildfire was a lightning caused wildfire discovered June 15th approximately 15 miles south-southwest of Grants, NM within the El Malpais National Monument. A favorable fire growth pattern allowed the fire to grow in its first few days surpassing 1,000 acres within 72 hours of its discovery. A turn to a wetter weather pattern abated fire growth on June 19th and onwards as the wildfire was then managed through June 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Encerrita Wildfire was a lightning start discovered within the El Malpais National Monument. The fire burned a total of 2,499 acres resulting in no damage or loss of life.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level high pressure continued to drift east towards west Texas. Southerly flow on the western flank of the upper level high helped bring up some mid-level moisture from Mexico. This mid-level moisture combined with deep daytime mixing and strong surface heating resulted in the development of high based showers and storms in the higher terrain midday June 7th. Outflow from this activity allowed more high based showers and storms to develop in the lower elevations across central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours. This resulted in brief high wind gusts at Albuquerque, Clines Corners, Clayton, and the Harry Mesonet in the White Sands Missile Range. High based shower and thunderstorm activity gradually dissipated around sunset thanks to the loss in instability and daytime heating. More high based showers and thunderstorms developed across southern and eastern NM during the afternoon hours of June 8th. The outflow from shower and storm activity west of Roswell produced several high wind gusts at the Roswell Air Center along with blowing dust across southern Chaves County. The blowing dust reduced visibility at the Roswell Air Center to 1/4 mile and shut down U.S. 285 near Midway and the Roswell Relief Route on the southwest side of town due to multiple vehicle accidents. High based showers and storms gradually dissipated around sunset.||Key Words: Thunderstorm Wind Gust, Dust Storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton Municipal Airport observed a high wind gust of 63 mph from the outflow on the leading edge of high based storms moving east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shallow shortwave and jet streak within the northwest to southeast oriented polar jet moved over New Mexico late December 29th into December 30th. These stronger winds aloft resulted in mountain wave activity overnight across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. One of these mountain waves crashed over the Clayton Municipal Airpark resulting in a brief 59 mph wind gust. A similar story was observed at the Hartley Ranch Wunderground station in southwest Harding County with a brief 59 mph gust before sunrise. Daytime mixing during the morning allowed the stronger winds aloft to mix down to the surface resulting in another high wind gust of 58 mph at a mesonet station east of Fort Sumner. Winds gradually tapered off during the afternoon and evening hours as the jet streak exited into the southern Great Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief 59 mph wind gust was observed at the Clayton Municipal Airpark. This was likely caused from a crashing mountain wave coming off the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a very dry and mild December across New Mexico. Some light snow fell across northeast New Mexico late December 9th into early December 10th and across the northern mountains Christmas Day and December 27th. No change in drought conditions were observed during the month. December ended up being the 2nd warmest December on record for Albuquerque and Roswell and the 5th warmest on record for Clayton.
